events.text: The first successful United States Pony Express run from St. Joseph, Missouri, to Sacramento, California, begins.

pages.title: Pony_Express
pages.description: 1860–1861 mail service in the United States
pages.extract: The Pony Express was an American express mail service that used relays of horse-mounted riders between Missouri and California. It was operated by the Central Overland California and Pikes Peak Express Company.
pages.extract_html: The Pony Express was an American express mail service that used relays of horse-mounted riders between Missouri and California. It was operated by the Central Overland California and Pikes Peak Express Company.

pages.title: St._Joseph,_Missouri
pages.description: City in and county seat of Buchanan County, Missouri, United States
pages.extract: St. Joseph is a city in and the county seat of Buchanan County, Missouri, United States. Located on the Missouri River, it is the principal city of the St. Joseph Metropolitan Statistical Area, which includes Buchanan, Andrew, and DeKalb counties in Missouri and Doniphan County, Kansas. As of the 2020 census, St. Joseph had a population of 72,473, making it the 8th most populous city in the state, and the 3rd most populous in Northwest Missouri. St. Joseph is located roughly 30 miles (48 km) north of the Kansas City, Missouri city limits and approximately 125 miles (201 km) south of Omaha, Nebraska.

pages.title: Sacramento,_California
pages.description: Capital city of California, United States
pages.extract: Sacramento is the capital city of the U.S. state of California. The county seat of Sacramento County, it is located at the confluence of the Sacramento and American Rivers in the Sacramento Valley. It is the fourth-most populous city in Northern California, sixth-most populous city in the state, and 35th-most populous city in the United States, with a population of 524,943 at the 2020 census. The Sacramento metropolitan area, with 2.46 million residents, is the 27th-largest metropolitan area in the country.

events.text: Rama IV is crowned King of Thailand after the death of his half-brother, Rama III.

pages.title: Mongkut
pages.extract: Mongkut, posthumously honoured as King Mongkut the Great, was the fourth king of Siam from the Chakri dynasty, titled Rama IV. He reigned from 1851 until his death in 1868.
pages.extract_html: Mongkut, posthumously honoured as King Mongkut the Great, was the fourth king of Siam from the Chakri dynasty, titled Rama IV. He reigned from 1851 until his death in 1868.

pages.title: Monarchy_of_Thailand
pages.extract: The monarchy of Thailand is the constitutional form of government of Thailand. The king of Thailand is the head of state and head of the ruling Chakri dynasty.
pages.extract_html: The monarchy of Thailand is the constitutional form of government of Thailand. The king of Thailand is the head of state and head of the ruling Chakri dynasty.

pages.title: Thailand
pages.extract: Thailand, officially the Kingdom of Thailand, and formerly known as Siam until 1939, is a country located in mainland Southeast Asia. It shares land borders with Myanmar to the west and northwest, Laos to the east and northeast, Cambodia to the southeast, and Malaysia to the south. Its maritime boundaries include the Gulf of Thailand and the Andaman Sea, as well as maritime borders with Vietnam, Indonesia, and India. Thailand has a population of nearly 66 million people and covers an area of approximately 513,115 km2. The country's capital and largest city is Bangkok.

pages.title: Rama_III
pages.extract: Nangklao, also known by his posthumous regnal name Rama III, was King of Siam from 1824 to his death in 1851. During his reign, he initiated the country's first cautious engagements with the West. Siam's territory also reached its greatest extent during this period.

events.text: Robert Walpole becomes, in effect, the first Prime Minister of Great Britain, though he himself denied that title.

pages.title: Robert_Walpole
pages.description: Prime Minister of Great Britain from 1721 to 1742
pages.extract: Robert Walpole, 1st Earl of Orford, known between 1725 and 1742 as Sir Robert Walpole, was a British Whig statesman who is generally regarded as the de facto first Prime Minister of Great Britain, serving from 1721 to 1742. His formal titles included First Lord of the Treasury, Chancellor of the Exchequer, and Leader of the House of Commons. He is the longest serving prime minister in UK history, with a tenure of over 20 years.

He is the longest serving prime minister in UK history, with a tenure of over 20 years.

pages.title: Prime_Minister_of_the_United_Kingdom
pages.displaytitle: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom

titles.normalized: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom
titles.display: Prime Minister of the United Kingdom

pages.description: Head of government of the United Kingdom
pages.extract: The prime minister of the United Kingdom is the head of government of the United Kingdom. The prime minister advises the sovereign on the exercise of much of the royal prerogative, chairs the Cabinet, and selects its ministers. Modern prime ministers hold office by virtue of their ability to command the confidence of the House of Commons, so they are invariably members of Parliament.

events.text: The janissaries revolt in response to the debasement of coins.

pages.title: Janissary
pages.description: Elite infantry units and standing army of the Ottoman Empire (active 1363–1826)
pages.extract: A janissary was a member of the elite infantry units that formed the Ottoman sultan's household troops. They were one of the first modern standing armies, and perhaps the first infantry force in the world to be equipped primarily with firearms, adopted during the reign of Murad II. The corps was established under either Orhan or Murad I, and dismantled by Mahmud II in 1826.

The corps was established under either Orhan or Murad I, and dismantled by Mahmud II in 1826.

pages.title: Beylerbeyi_event
pages.extract: Beylerbeyi event was a revolt in the Ottoman Empire in response to currency debasement in April 1589, during the reign of Murat III.
pages.extract_html: Beylerbeyi event was a revolt in the Ottoman Empire in response to currency debasement in April 1589, during the reign of Murat III.

events.text: The second of two treaties making up the Peace of Cateau-Cambrésis is signed, ending the Italian Wars.

pages.title: Treaty_of_Cateau-Cambrésis
pages.description: Peace treaty that ended the Italian Wars
pages.extract: The Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis in April 1559 ended the Italian Wars (1494–1559). It consisted of two separate treaties, one between England and France on 2 April, and another between France and Spain on 3 April. Although he was not a signatory, both were approved by Emperor Ferdinand I, since many of the territorial exchanges concerned states within the Holy Roman Empire.
pages.extract_html: The Treaty of Cateau-Cambrésis in April 1559 ended the Italian Wars (1494–1559). Although he was not a signatory, both were approved by Emperor Ferdinand I, since many of the territorial exchanges concerned states within the Holy Roman Empire.

pages.title: Italian_Wars
pages.extract: The Italian Wars were a series of conflicts fought between 1494 and 1559 in the Italian Peninsula, with subsidiary theatres in Flanders, the Rhineland and Mediterranean Sea. A product of the long-running French–Habsburg rivalry, its primary belligerents were France versus the Holy Roman Empire and Spain, supported by numerous Italian states at different stages, along with England, and the Ottoman Empire.

events.text: The Patriarchate of Friûl, the first Friulian state, is created.

pages.title: Friuli
pages.extract: Friuli is a historical region of northeast Italy. The region is marked by its separate regional and ethnic identity predominantly tied to the Friulians, who speak the Friulian language. It comprises the major part of the autonomous region Friuli-Venezia Giulia, i.e. the administrative provinces of Udine, Pordenone, and Gorizia, excluding Trieste.

events.text: Edward the Confessor is crowned King of England.

pages.title: Edward_the_Confessor
pages.extract: Edward the Confessor was King of the English from 1042 until his death in 1066. He was the last reigning monarch of the House of Wessex.
pages.extract_html: Edward the Confessor was King of the English from 1042 until his death in 1066. He was the last reigning monarch of the House of Wessex.

pages.title: Monarchy_of_the_United_Kingdom
pages.extract: The monarchy of the United Kingdom, commonly referred to as the British monarchy, is the form of government used by the United Kingdom by which a hereditary monarch reigns as the head of state, with their powers regulated by the British constitution. The term may also refer to the role of the royal family within the UK's broader political structure. The monarch since 8 September 2022 is King Charles III, who ascended the throne on the death of his mother, Queen Elizabeth II.

events.text: Polyeuctus of Constantinople is elected as patriarch of Constantinople.

pages.title: Polyeuctus_of_Constantinople
pages.description: Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople from 956 to 970
pages.extract: Polyeuctus of Constantinople was Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople (956–970). His orthodox feast is on 5 February.
pages.extract_html: Polyeuctus of Constantinople was Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople (956–970). His orthodox feast is on 5 February.

pages.title: Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople
pages.description: First among equals of leaders in the Orthodox Church
pages.extract: The Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople is the archbishop of Constantinople and primus inter pares among the heads of the several autocephalous churches that comprise the Eastern Orthodox Church. The ecumenical patriarch is regarded as the representative and spiritual leader of the Eastern Orthodox Christians worldwide. The term ecumenical in the title is a historical reference to the Ecumene, a Greek designation for the civilised world, i.e. the Roman Empire, and it stems from Canon 28 of the Council of Chalcedon.
pages.extract_html: The Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople is the archbishop of Constantinople and primus inter pares among the heads of the several autocephalous churches that comprise the Eastern Orthodox Church. events.text: Maya king Yuknoom Yich'aak K'ahk' assumes the crown of Calakmul.

pages.title: Maya_civilization
pages.extract: The Maya civilization was a Mesoamerican civilization that existed from antiquity to the early modern period. It is known by its ancient temples and glyphs (script). The Maya script is the most sophisticated and highly developed writing system in the pre-Columbian Americas. The civilization is also noted for its art, architecture, mathematics, calendar, and astronomical system.

pages.title: Yuknoom_Yichʼaak_Kʼahkʼ
pages.description: 7th-century king of the Mayan kingdom of Kaan
pages.extract: Yuknoom Yichʼaak Kʼahkʼ or Yuknoom Ixquiac was a Maya king of the Kaan kingdom, which had its capital at Calakmul during the Classic Period of Mesoamerican chronology.
pages.extract_html: Yuknoom Yichʼaak Kʼahkʼ or Yuknoom Ixquiac was a Maya king of the Kaan kingdom, which had its capital at Calakmul during the Classic Period of Mesoamerican chronology.

pages.title: Calakmul
pages.description: Ancient Mayan city in Campeche, Mexico
pages.extract: Calakmul is a Maya archaeological site in the Mexican state of Campeche, deep in the jungles of the greater Petén Basin region. It is 35 kilometres (22 mi) from the Guatemalan border. Calakmul was one of the largest and most powerful Mayan cities.
pages.extract_html: Calakmul is a Maya archaeological site in the Mexican state of Campeche, deep in the jungles of the greater Petén Basin region. It is 35 kilometres (22 mi) from the Guatemalan border. Calakmul was one of the largest and most powerful Mayan cities.

holidays.text:
Christian feast day:
Agape, Chionia, and Irene

pages.title: Agape,_Chionia,_and_Irene
pages.extract: Saint Agape, Saint Chionia and Saint Irene were sisters and Christian saints from Aquileia, martyred at Thessalonica in 304 AD. Agape and Chionia were charged with refusing to eat sacrificial offerings, whilst Irene was killed for keeping Christian books in violation of existing law. All were condemned to be burned alive.
pages.extract_html: Saint Agape, Saint Chionia and Saint Irene were sisters and Christian saints from Aquileia, martyred at Thessalonica in 304 AD. Agape and Chionia were charged with refusing to eat sacrificial offerings, whilst Irene was killed for keeping Christian books in violation of existing law. All were condemned to be burned alive.

pages.title: Burgundofara
pages.extract: Burgundofara, also Saint Fara or Fare, was the founder and first abbess of the Abbey of Faremoutiers.
pages.extract_html: Burgundofara, also Saint Fara or Fare, was the founder and first abbess of the Abbey of Faremoutiers.

holidays.text:
Christian feast day:
Joseph the Hymnographer

pages.title: Joseph_the_Hymnographer
pages.extract: Joseph the Hymnographer was a Greek monk of the ninth century. He is regarded as one of the greatest liturgical poets and hymnographers of the Eastern Orthodox Church. He is also known for his confession of the Orthodox Faith in opposition to Iconoclasm.
pages.extract_html: Joseph the Hymnographer was a Greek monk of the ninth century. He is regarded as one of the greatest liturgical poets and hymnographers of the Eastern Orthodox Church. He is also known for his confession of the Orthodox Faith in opposition to Iconoclasm.

pages.title: Luigi_Scrosoppi
pages.extract: Luigi Scrosoppi was an Italian priest of the Catholic Church who founded the Sisters of Providence of Saint Cajetan of Thiene. He was canonized in 2001.
pages.extract_html: Luigi Scrosoppi was an Italian priest of the Catholic Church who founded the Sisters of Providence of Saint Cajetan of Thiene. He was canonized in 2001.

holidays.text:
Christian feast day:
Piotr Edward Dankowski

pages.title: Piotr_Edward_Dankowski
pages.extract: Piotr Edward Dańkowski is a Polish Catholic saint who is one of the 108 Martyrs of World War II beatified by Pope John Paul II. He is the patron saint of clerics and priests of the Archdiocese of Krakow.

holidays.text:
Christian feast day:
Richard of Chichester

pages.title: Richard_of_Chichester
pages.extract: Richard of Chichester, also known as Richard de Wych, is a saint who was Bishop of Chichester.
